title: World Silver Survey 2026
source_institution: The Silver Institute (researched and produced by Metals Focus)
core_thesis: The 2025–2026 silver price surge is rooted in a persistent structural deficit and tightening global liquidity rather than a speculative anomaly. The market recorded a sizeable deficit in 2025 (around 40 Moz) as mine supply and scrap failed to keep pace with robust industrial demand and strong investment inflows, including ETPs and physical bullion. Above‑ground inventories in key hubs (especially London) were drawn down, lease rates spiked, and regional liquidity became tight, amplifying price moves. The report projects a sixth consecutive annual deficit in 2026, with continued vulnerability from low visible stocks and elevated lease rates. It argues that unless there is either (a) a significant change in industrial usage (e.g., substitution in photovoltaics and electronics), or (b) a material rebound in mine and recycling supply, silver will remain in structural deficit, keeping prices elevated and volatility high as liquidity and inventory constraints interact with investor flows.

title: How Will Silver Prices Fare in 2026?
core_thesis: Silver’s explosive 2025–early‑2026 rally was driven by an exceptionally tight physical market layered on top of multi‑year supply deficits and outsized investment demand, but the resulting price level embeds substantial froth and is vulnerable to correction. Silver nearly doubled in 2025 (roughly a 130% gain from about \$29/oz to above \$70/oz), aided by strong industrial offtake, heavy ETF inflows closely tracking price, and tariff‑related disruptions (notably U.S. Section 232) that redirected metal from London to New York and exacerbated local illiquidity. J.P. Morgan forecasts silver to average about \$81/oz in 2026 and end the year near \$85/oz—more than double its 2025 average—while warning that silver will continue to trade as a high‑beta expression of the gold trade: closely correlated to gold but with more pronounced boom‑bust swings. The report’s stance is constructive but tactical: current prices already reflect tight fundamentals and strong sentiment, so investors are advised to re‑enter or add exposure after episodes of normalization and froth unwinding rather than chase near‑term spikes.

title: The silver surge
source_institution: Standard Chartered (Wealth Management – Global CIO Office)
core_thesis: Silver’s 2025–early‑2026 surge is best understood as a haven‑driven catch‑up rally to gold, turbocharged by macro uncertainty and positioning, and now largely pricing in the fundamental case—implying a more tactical, level‑sensitive approach going forward. From early 2025 to late December, silver rose about 171%, hitting an intraday high near \$80/oz and closing the year around \$72/oz, while the gold–silver ratio collapsed to roughly 56 versus a long‑term average near 68, and ETF holdings as well as withdrawals from LBMA vaults signaled tightening slack. The report argues that further substantial upside from here requires either higher gold prices or an even larger compression in the gold–silver ratio. Based on a 12‑month gold target of about \$4,800/oz, returning the ratio to its 30‑year mean implies silver around \$71/oz, while re‑testing extreme historical lows in the ratio could theoretically point to \$150+/oz; however, much of that mean‑reversion is deemed already priced in. Standard Chartered concludes that near‑term risk–reward is less compelling after the parabolic move—recommending adding on pullbacks rather than chasing strength—while maintaining a medium‑term positive bias given ongoing macro tailwinds and strong momentum.
